metadata.summary: Every repo with shell scripts, Dockerfiles, YAML, GitHub Actions, or Terraform MUST run shellcheck, hadolint, yamllint, actionlint, and tflint at the same hook tier as code linters.

content.title: Infrastructure-as-Code Lint Floor (shellcheck, hadolint, yamllint, actionlint, tflint)
content.summary: Every repository with shell scripts, Dockerfiles, Compose files, GitHub Actions workflows, Kubernetes manifests, Terraform, or YAML configuration MUST run a per-format infrastructure linter at the same hook tier as code linters. The minimum set is shellcheck for *.sh and bash-shebanged files, hadolint for any Dockerfile*, yamllint for .yaml/.yml, actionlint for .github/workflows/, and tflint (plus terraform validate) for HCL. Findings block at the same level as code-lint findings; hooks run pre-commit on the staged file set and CI runs the whole tree once per PR. AI agents fix flagged issues at the source (pin apt versions, drop latest tags, quote shell variables) rather than disabling the rule.

why.p: App-code linters (ruff, biome) cannot see infrastructure code. Production failures routinely originate in shell quoting (Shellshock-class), Dockerfile rootful images and unpinned latest tags, GitHub Actions privilege misuses (especially pull_request_target paired with checkout of head SHA), Compose port leaks and Terraform plan drift. The IaC linter set is mature and maintained: shellcheck is the de-facto Haskell-based shell analyzer (MIT), hadolint codifies the Docker best-practice catalog, yamllint guards spec-compliant YAML, actionlint is the GitHub-published linter that also runs shellcheck against run: blocks, and tflint adds provider-aware Terraform rules above terraform validate. Skipping any of them lets a known class of outage land at runtime instead of pre-merge.

list.item: Every repository that contains at least one Dockerfile, shell script, GitHub Actions workflow, Kubernetes manifest, Terraform file or non-trivial YAML config (i.e., effectively every production repo).
Polyglot monorepos where app-code linters skip the IaC tree entirely.
Repos with self-hosted CI or community-contributed workflows where action injection is a real attack surface.
Hardening sprints after an incident traceable to a shell or Dockerfile bug.
Pure prose / Markdown / asset repos with no scripts, no CI workflows, no IaC — there is nothing for these tools to see.
Throwaway prototype repos where the operational surface is hand-managed and never deployed automatically.
Repos under active migration where IaC is being rewritten — schedule a one-shot baseline reset, then turn the hooks back on.

rule.statement: The pre-commit config of every repo containing the matching files MUST include: `shellcheck` on every `*.sh` and bash-shebanged file (-S style minimum), `hadolint` on every `Dockerfile*` (default ruleset), `yamllint` on every `.yaml`/`.yml` (with at minimum the `relaxed` profile), `actionlint` on every `.github/workflows/*.yml`, and `tflint --recursive` plus `terraform validate` on every Terraform module. CI MUST run the whole-tree variant of each tool on PR open. Findings of severity `error` or higher MUST block both the local commit and the merge check.
rule.rationale: This mapping is the intersection of the OpenSSF Scorecard "infrastructure-as-code" metric, GitHub's own actionlint deployment guide, and the Docker best-practices catalog hadolint encodes. Skipping a tier (e.g., "we'll add shellcheck later") leaves a known class of bug — Shellshock-style quoting, rootful Docker images, action-injection workflows — uncaught at the cheapest gate.

example.title: Full IaC pre-commit hook set
example.code: # .pre-commit-config.yaml fragment - repo: https://github.com/koalaman/shellcheck-precommit rev: v0.10.0 hooks: [{id: shellcheck}] - repo: https://github.com/hadolint/hadolint rev: v2.13.0-beta hooks: [{id: hadolint-docker}] - repo: https://github.com/adrienverge/yamllint rev: v1.35.1 hooks: [{id: yamllint, args: [-c=.yamllint.yml]}] - repo: https://github.com/rhysd/actionlint rev: v1.7.4 hooks: [{id: actionlint}]
example.note: Each hook is bound to its format's glob via the upstream hook definition; agents see all five run on the staged set.

example.title: Relying on docker build to catch Dockerfile issues
example.code: # Just rely on docker build to fail later on: [pull_request] jobs: build: steps: - run: docker build .
example.note: By the time `docker build` rejects the file, the agent has already pushed three follow-up commits; pre-merge hadolint catches the issue 60 seconds after the diff is written.

antipattern.description: Running only `terraform validate` and skipping `tflint` because validate "already runs in CI."
antipattern.reason: `terraform validate` only checks syntax and provider schema. tflint adds rules like `aws_instance_invalid_type`, deprecated-syntax flags, and per-provider best-practice checks that validate cannot see. Both are needed.

rule.statement: An AI agent that triggers a `shellcheck`, `hadolint`, `yamllint`, `actionlint`, or `tflint` finding MUST modify the offending file to satisfy the rule. The agent MUST NOT add `# shellcheck disable=...`, `# hadolint ignore=...`, `# yamllint disable-line`, `# actionlint shellcheck`-disable, or `tflint:disable` UNLESS: (a) the suppression cites the specific rule code, (b) a one-line justification is included in the same diff, and (c) a tracking issue (URL or ticket ID) is named in the comment for sunset. Blanket file-level disables are forbidden.
rule.rationale: Disable comments accumulate forever and turn the linter advisory. The Anthropic Claude Code "constitution-driven" pattern and faion-net's own feedback_granular_commits.md memory both treat suppression with sunset tracking as the contract — an issue anchors the future cleanup. Without that anchor, the comment is technical debt with no cost owner.

example.title: Fix the source instead of disabling the rule
example.code:
# Before: shellcheck SC2086 "Double-quote to prevent globbing" files=$(ls *.txt) for f in $files; do cat $f; done
# After: the fix. for f in *.txt; do cat "$f"; done
example.note: Glob loop instead of `ls` parsing; quoted variable; the underlying class of bug (whitespace-in-filenames) is gone.

example.title: Disabling rules without justification or tracking
example.code: # shellcheck disable=SC2086,SC2046 files=$(ls *.txt) for f in $files; do cat $f; done
example.note: Two rules silenced with no justification, no issue link, no sunset. The bug class is now invisible to the linter forever.

antipattern.description: Adding `--no-fail` or `|| true` to the linter invocation in CI to "stop blocking us during the migration."
antipattern.reason: That converts the gate to advisory across the entire repo, not just the offending file. The right move is a per-file allowlist with a tracking issue and a date.

template.purpose: .pre-commit-config.yaml snippet wiring shellcheck, hadolint, yamllint, actionlint, tflint to the right file globs.
GitHub Actions matrix job running the full IaC linter set whole-tree on PR open.

see-also.link: shellcheck — the canonical shell static analyzer.
hadolint — Dockerfile linter encoding Docker best-practices.
actionlint — GitHub Actions workflow linter (also runs shellcheck on run:).
yamllint — YAML format and style checker.
shellcheck rule catalog with concrete fixes per code.
